data.title: China warns of ‘honey trap’ risks in defence sector after official jailed for espionage
data.description:
Chinese state security authorities recently warned of “honey trap” risks in the defence and technology sectors, citing a case in which a public official was blackmailed into surrendering state secrets following a trip abroad.
A social media account affiliated with the National Administration of State Secrets Protection published an article on Wednesday stating that personnel in the military industry were becoming targets of honey traps by foreign intelligence agencies and provided details of a related case.
According to the account called Baomiguan, a project manager surnamed Guo from a mainland military enterprise was on a work trip to a foreign city to accept a project. While abroad, a spy posing as a “business representative” approached Guo via deliberately arranged social occasions.
“This seemingly accidental encounter was actually a honey trap orchestrated by a foreign intelligence agency,” the article said.


After Guo had sexual relations with a woman working for the spy’s organisation, it threatened to expose Guo’s affair and successfully recruited him, it reported.
Subsequently, Guo accepted tasks assigned by the organisation to help in recruiting a Chinese national surnamed Chen, a technical expert from a company, and another Chinese national surnamed Li, a staff member.
The three provided the organisation with internal information and classified details about their military-industrial enterprise, according to the article.
“Throughout the intelligence exchange, Guo acted as the on-site interpreter,” it said.
China’s law on guarding state secrets has a three-tier system for classified information: confidential, secret and top secret.
In this case, 12 documents reportedly leaked by Guo were categorised as confidential, and two documents provided by Chen and Li were also confidential.
The article said Guo was ultimately sentenced to 10 years in prison for espionage, and Chen and Li were also held criminally liable.
It did not say where the case took place or when.

This is not the first time that security agencies have warned against honey traps during trips abroad, but details are rarely revealed in such cases involving the military industry.
The article cautioned against shortcomings in internal security management within some organisations, particularly in perfunctory visit abroad reviews and a lack of oversight abroad.
It also offered suggestions to improve supervision, management and preventive education for personnel travelling abroad.
“[We should] focus on enhancing the awareness and political discernment of personnel visiting abroad through typical cases and interactive experiential education,” the article stated.
It further proposed implementing full-cycle management for personnel travelling abroad, from pre-trip reviews and supervision abroad to post-return observation.
It also suggested strengthening policies that require state employees to declare their relationships abroad.
China’s Ministry of State Security has repeatedly told mainland officials, students and tourists to be wary of recruitment traps abroad set by foreign intelligence agencies amid Beijing’s fierce competition with Washington and its Western allies.
In May 2024, the ministry disclosed a case in which a Chinese provincial government official was targeted in a honey trap while travelling abroad.

data.title: Turkey, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia plan defence pact as US-Iran row threatens Middle East
data.description:
As tensions between the United States and Iran threaten to plunge the Middle East into chaos again, regional military powers Turkey and Pakistan have revealed they are planning a tripartite defence partnership with Saudi Arabia.
Thursday’s disclosure comes as Iran’s neighbours await clarity on US President Donald Trump’s announcement of a 25 per cent tariff on the Islamic Republic’s trading partners earlier in the week – a measure which threatens to disrupt supply chains across the region.
The proposed trilateral agreement “is something that is already in the pipeline”, Pakistan’s defence production minister Raza Hayat Harraj told Reuters news agency on Thursday.
It was separate to nuclear-armed Pakistan and Saudi Arabia’s mutual defence agreement, signed in September, soon after Israeli warplanes attacked Hamas negotiators in Qatar, he said, indicating that the proposed deal was not likely to include Nato-like commitments to come to each other’s aid in the event of conflict.
Turkish foreign minister Hakan Fidan, addressing a press conference in Ankara on Thursday, confirmed that negotiations with regional states – which he declined to name – have been under way since early last year.

Although an agreement had yet to be reached, Fidan said the proposed security mechanism would act as a shared platform for “all regional nations”, whose issues could be resolved if they could be “be sure of each other”.
Experts say the pact reflects concerns about Washington’s increasingly erratic behaviour, which threatens to disrupt both the region’s fragile power balance and trades in energy, petrochemicals and food.
Strategically, it would signal a “more self-reliant security geometry linking the Gulf, Anatolia and South Asia”, said Andreas Krieg, an associate professor of defence studies at King’s College London.
A three-way deal would also “strengthen deterrence narratives aimed at Iran and help Riyadh diversify partners beyond the West”, the risk analyst told This Week In Asia.
Krieg predicted that it was more likely to “resemble a cooperation and capability-building package than a mutual-defence clause” – as any such clause would face legal and parliamentary scrutiny, and “invite external pressure from Washington and, indirectly, Israel”.
Different priorities
However, translating shared political intent into an operationally meaningful framework, would be a challenge because all three nations “have overlapping interests, but different threat prioritisation and risk tolerances”, Krieg said.
Riyadh is focused on air and missile defence, maritime security and Iran, while Ankara’s priorities are shaped by Syria, the Eastern Mediterranean and its Nato relationship. On the other hand, Islamabad must constantly calibrate around India, Iran and domestic counterterrorism demands.
Another friction point is intelligence sharing and command-and-control. “Partners will be cautious about sensitive data, rules of engagement and who has access to what systems,” Krieg said.
“Defence industrial politics [also] matter,” he added, because co-production, offsets, pricing and technology transfer “are where deals often stall”.


Like all of Iran’s neighbours, the three countries are worried about the potential impact of Trump’s tariffs, unveiled on Monday.
At a conference in Abu Dhabi on Tuesday, the UAE’s minister of foreign trade Thani al-Zeyoudi said the levy could affect supplies of Iranian produce and other goods to the Emirates.
Iran is the UAE’s second largest trading partner. It imports large quantities of Iranian produce as feedstock for its flourishing processed food industry.
Dubai was looking into how the tariffs could “affect the prices on the consumer and how much they’re paying to have the alternative”, Zeyoudi said.
In Pakistan, imports of staples like tomatoes from Iran soften the inflationary impact of seasonal supply shortfalls. Alongside India, Pakistan is a major producer of basmati rice, the backbone of Iranian cuisine.
In turn, all three countries act as an import lifeline for Iran’s sanctions-bound economy and the country’s 90 million consumers whose plight – exacerbated by Tehran’s gross mismanagement and widespread corruption – has sparked the most widespread, violent protests since the 1979 revolution.
If Iran’s ability to trade is affected, its currency, the rial, may crash “even further than the record lows” that triggered the recent protests, according to Farzan Sabet, a managing researcher of the Global Governance Centre in Geneva.
This could create a “massive surge in inflation”, he said in social media posts on Tuesday.
‘A bit vague’
In the face of such pressure, analysts expect Iran’s major trading partners to push back against the tariff, both for economic reasons and a discomfort at the prospect of Iran being destabilised by sustained US air strikes.
“Keeping the Iranian economy afloat has been a strategic priority for the UAE since 2021,” said Esfandyar Batmanghelidj, CEO of the Bourse and Bazaar Foundation, a think tank based in London.
“The tariffs won’t change that calculus” as they seemed “neither enforceable nor consequential, and everyone is going to forget about it in a week,” Batmanghelidj said on social media on Tuesday.
Turkish policy expert Bill Park also doubted that the new US tariff would “actually be imposed, or last for long”.

“Quite when and how they would be imposed is a bit vague,” the defence studies lecturer at King’s College London told This Week In Asia. “It’s Trump mouthing off again.”
Turkey and Iran last month announced their desire to boost bilateral trade and revive their 2015 preferential trade agreement. They also signed a long-term deal to extend Iranian gas exports to Turkey.
“Both are committed to expanding trade but in reality trade between the two has fluctuated, partly through the impact of sanctions, partly through Iran’s economic dysfunctionality,” Park said.
Turkey and Iran would “probably find workarounds”, he said – as would Tehran’s other neighbours.

data.title: Hong Kong marathons throughout the years – SCMP archive
data.description:
The Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon attracts tens of thousands of people from all over the world every year but the sporting event had humble beginnings in the city.
When Hong Kong held its first international marathon in 1969, there were only 28 participants.
In this collection of pictures, we look at how the annual running event has grown throughout the years.

data.title: Hong Kong government’s cargo-tracking digital platform launched with 2,300 firms
data.description:
A digital logistics platform developed by the Hong Kong government that offers real-time cargo tracking has been launched, with some 2,300 companies registered with the system.
Secretary for Transport and Logistics Mable Chan said on Friday that the new Port Community System would streamline transport data and improve transparency for users.
The system integrates sea, land and air data and uses artificial intelligence (AI) and blockchain technology to provide instantaneous cargo tracking services, which Chan said were “essential” for the logistics industry.
At the launch, Chan said the port system aimed to strengthen Hong Kong’s role as an international financial, trade and maritime centre through digitalisation.
“This is a great result from the combined effort of government and industry working together,” Chan said. “What the government does must be practical for the industry … and must grasp the industry’s needs and comprehensively plan around them.”
The logistics chief said that the bureau would continue to work with the Hong Kong Monetary Authority’s “Project CargoX” – which centralises logistics data – and provide cargo and trade data from the port system, similar to the airport’s cargo platform.
The data could help small and medium-sized enterprises secure financing more quickly, she said.

The bureau would also work with Hong Kong customs to digitalise the cargo detention notification process and provide industry players with “faster and more transparent information”, improving customs clearance efficiency.
The Port Community System is part of the government’s smart port development strategy to enhance competitiveness and maintain Hong Kong’s position as a regional transshipment hub.
It aims to connect relevant units in the local logistics supply chain, while linking with international ports and commercial shipping data platforms.
The system’s first prototype was introduced in an industry trial in 2023. Funding for the system amounting to HK$210 million (US$26.9 million) was greenlit by the Legislative Council’s Finance Committee last March.

data.title: How tonka beans can add a touch of spice to your desserts (and sex life)
data.description:
The tonka bean is not the most visually appealing spice – the black, shrivelled seeds look rather like what a large rat would leave behind.
My first encounter with it was not promising. A pastry chef mentioned that he had made some tonka bean ice cream. He showed me a bagful of the seeds and, being curious, I popped one into my mouth. The bitter intensity of the bean was almost overwhelming.
I did some research and found that the tonka bean contains coumarin, a chemical that can cause liver damage if consumed in high doses (fortunately, one seed is not considered a high dose). For this reason, the bean is banned in the United States.
When used in desserts, its flavour becomes much more subtle and complex. It is often compared to that of the vanilla bean, but with spicy notes.

Because they are very aromatic, tonka beans, which are harvested from trees in South America, are also used in perfumes and essential oil, in which they are said to work as an aphrodisiac and ease depression.
In cooking, grate the tonka bean on a fine-toothed rasp grater. It goes well with creamy, milky desserts such as ice cream (substitute tonka for vanilla bean) and custards. You can also mix the grated tonka bean into cookie dough or cake batter.
This content first appeared in Post Magazine in 2013.

data.title: Malaysian man arrested for forcing son, 10, to shoplift regularly
data.description:
A Malaysian man previously convicted of child abuse has been arrested again on suspicion of forcing his son, 10, to shoplift daily necessities.
Police in Bahau, in Negeri Sembilan state’s Jempol district, were alerted to what initially appeared to be a simple case of theft on Wednesday, The Sun newspaper reported. Staff from a wholesale supermarket had caught the boy trying to steal a 200-gram pack of instant coffee.
But officers soon uncovered a troubling pattern of child exploitation, Jempol police deputy chief R. Premkumar told Bernama news agency.
The boy’s 34-year-old father was believed to have been forcing him to shoplift items such as Nescafe and Milo products from outlets in the area.
“Initial investigations revealed that the suspect frequently directed and forced his eldest son to steal daily necessities from several other business premises previously,” Premkumar said.
“If the victim refused, the suspect would scold him and threaten to inflict physical harm on the child.”
Background checks revealed the father had seven previous convictions for criminal activities and drug-related offences. One of them was for child abuse, indicating that it was not the first time he had harmed children, district police chief Norhisham Mustapar told the Chinese language newspaper China Press.
“He has been on the police’s wanted list since 2021,” Norhisham said.


The man was placed in remand on Thursday for further investigation and may face charges of theft, abetting a crime and child abuse. Meanwhile, the boy’s mother has been given custody to remove him from the abusive situation.
In comments under a post about the case, social media users said they were “speechless” over the “trauma for the boy”. “I really don’t understand. Just why would he do this?” one said in reference to the father. “He’s just a kid,” another wrote.
According to Malaysia’s welfare department, nearly 5,000 children were reportedly in need of protection from abuse in 2016 alone. “Hyperactivity, disability and financial problems are just some of the everyday issues that have driven caregivers to abuse and neglect children,” Unicef said in a Malaysia report.
In May last year, after Penang’s welfare department said there had been over 4,000 child abuse cases in the state since 2010, P. Sundramoorthy, a criminologist at Universiti Sains Malaysia, told the New Straits Times that economic hardship played a big part.
“Families facing poverty and unemployment often endure elevated stress levels, which can result in neglect or abuse. The Family Stress Model explains how economic disadvantage can lead to psychological distress in caregivers, which in turn affects parenting behaviours and increases the risk of child maltreatment,” he said.
Mental health issues, lack of parenting knowledge and skills, family instability and parental substance abuse are some of the other stressors, according to Sundramoorthy.
Malaysia’s Child Act 2001 criminalises various forms of child abuse and exploitation, including physical harm and threats, forcing children to beg or engage in criminal activity, and exposing the young to situations that are harmful to their physical or emotional well-being.
Penalties vary but mostly consist of a fine, imprisonment or caning.

data.title: South Korea’s Yoon gets 5 years in prison for obstructing justice during martial law bid
data.description:
A South Korean court on Friday sentenced former president Yoon Suk-yeol to five years in prison after finding him guilty of obstruction of justice during his disastrous martial law declaration and in its chaotic aftermath, the first in a series of verdicts for the disgraced ex-leader.
Yoon briefly suspended civilian rule in South Korea for the first time in more than four decades on December 3, 2024, prompting massive protests and a showdown in parliament.
Now ousted from power, he faces multiple trials for actions taken during that debacle and in the turmoil that followed.
Among them are charges he committed obstruction of justice by allegedly excluding cabinet members from a martial law planning meeting as well as blocking investigators from detaining him.
The case mainly centres on accusations that Yoon instructed presidential security personnel to block investigators from entering his residential compound to execute an arrest warrant in January last year.
The first attempt failed after authorities encountered buses parked to block the entrance. They later succeeded in entering the compound and bringing Yoon in for questioning.
The sentencing comes days after prosecutors in a separate case demanded Yoon be sentenced to death for his role as the “ringleader of an insurrection” in orchestrating the imposition of martial law.
They argued Yoon deserved the severest possible punishment as he had shown “no remorse” for actions that threatened “constitutional order and democracy”.
If he is found guilty, it is highly unlikely the sentence will actually be carried out – South Korea has had an unofficial moratorium on executions since 1997.


Yoon was seen smiling in court as the prosecutors demanded the punishment.
And the former leader and top prosecutor has remained defiant, saying his martial law declaration was a lawful exercise of his presidential authority.
In closing remarks on Tuesday, he insisted the “exercise of a president’s constitutional emergency powers to protect the nation and uphold the constitutional order cannot be deemed an act of insurrection”.
He accused the then opposition party of having imposed an “unconstitutional dictatorship” through their control of the legislature.
“There was no other option but to awaken the people who are the sovereign.”
The court is scheduled to rule on the insurrection charges on February 19.
Yoon also faces a separate trial on charges of aiding the enemy over allegations he ordered drone flights over North Korea to bolster his case for declaring martial law.

data.title: Chinese woman swallows raw fish gallbladder, hoping to relieve headache, ends up in ICU
data.description:
A woman from eastern China ended up in the intensive care unit (ICU) hours after swallowing a raw fish gallbladder, believing it would cure her headache.
The 50-year-old woman, surnamed Liu, from Jiangsu province, adhered to the folk remedy that claimed raw fish gallbladders could clear heat, detoxify the body, and alleviate a persistent migraine that had been troubling her.
On the morning of December 14, Liu bought a 2.5kg grass carp at the market, extracted the gallbladder at home, and swallowed it raw.

Just two hours later, she began experiencing vomiting, diarrhoea, and stomach pain. Her family quickly took her to the hospital, where she was diagnosed with fish gallbladder poisoning and acute hepatic failure.
She was promptly transferred to the ICU at the Affiliated Hospital of Jiangsu University.
The medical team provided her with plasma exchange treatment and continuous renal replacement therapy, a slow blood purification method.
Five days later, Liu’s condition improved, and she was discharged from the hospital.
The case was reported by the hospital on January 7.
Doctor Hu Zhenkui stated that fish gallbladder is “even more toxic than arsenic.” He explained that only a few grams can be enough to poison a person, and gallbladders from fish weighing 5kg or more could be fatal.

He noted that the gallbladders contain toxins that can damage the liver and kidneys, potentially leading to acute liver failure and kidney failure. In severe cases, these toxins can cause shock, cerebral haemorrhage and even death.
Hu further cautioned that fish gallbladders, whether cooked or soaked in liquor – practices some believe enhance their nutritional value – remain toxic.
The hospital also advised individuals not to trust superstitious folk remedies.
Liu is not alone; there have been many cases of hospitalisation due to the consumption of raw fish gallbladders.
Patients have reported believing that this organ could help clear heat, improve eyesight and detoxify the liver.
Some even think that the gallbladder’s bitterness indicates its medicinal effectiveness.

In one case from central China’s Hubei province, a woman suffered from diarrhoea for three days after consuming a raw fish gallbladder, convinced it was a sign that her heat was being cleared.
By the time she sought medical help, serious damage had already occurred to her liver and kidneys.
“This is not health maintenance but suicide,” remarked one online observer.
“Always trust in medical advice from doctors rather than folk remedies,” advised another.
“My mum fed me raw fish gallbladders several times when I was a kid. I’m fortunate to be alive today,” shared a third.

data.title: China’s US envoy says world faces ‘stark choice’ as 2026 opens with instability
data.description:
China’s top diplomat in the United States delivered a sharply worded rebuke on Thursday over US President Donald Trump’s tumultuous approach to global relations, laying out a stark choice between a world informed by China-led stability and one battered by Washington.
Chinese ambassador Xie Feng’s comments to the 1,000-member China General Chamber of Commerce come two weeks into Trump’s second year of his latest term in the White House.
The past fortnight has already seen the Trump administration abduct Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro; threaten Greenland, Mexico, Colombia and Cuba; pull the US out of 61 international organisations; threaten Iran; defend an immigration agent who shot an unarmed mother; suspend immigrant visas from 75 countries; and file a criminal investigation against the US central bank chairman.
“We are just 15 days into the year 2026, but the year already has seen black swans and grey rhinos one after the other. Instability and uncertainty are raging all around,” Xie said. “Where will the world be heading? Humanity is facing a grave choice. Peace or war. Openness or isolation. Cooperation or confrontation.”
Black swan and grey rhino events refer to highly improbable, unpredictable developments that carry widespread, far-reaching consequences.


Xie addressed the chamber members hours after his embassy blasted the US over a “so-called agreement” worth up to US$500 billion to bring critical Taiwanese tech manufacturing to the US, a deal it “strongly deplored” and called on Washington to revoke.
It was also weeks after Washington approved its largest-ever arms package to the self-governed island.
Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping are scheduled to meet in Beijing in April and the growing number of irritants, following a truce established when the two met in South Korea in October, threaten to further fray ties.
The White House and the US State Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Xie, who has served as US ambassador since 2023, rarely used Trump’s name in the address but his intent was clear.
“The Cold War mentality has staged a comeback with power trampling rules, might challenging what is right and hegemony and bully replacing dialogue and consultation,” he said.
“As a result, the international order is facing a systematic shock, which risks sharply rising, threatening to drag the world back into the jungle.”
The seasoned diplomat sought to offer a vision of a world built on international justice with Beijing as a model. This comes as public polls and global indices show a rapid decline in Washington’s reputation and soft power, even as China notches up.
“China firmly stands on the right side of history and the progress of human civilisation,” Xie said. “While might may rule the moment, right prevails … Achieving international cooperation, sovereign equality, settling international disputes by peaceful means, are not outdated.”


The ambassador, a Jiangsu native and former commissioner of Hong Kong, spent a significant portion of his speech hammering home the “Taiwan question”, citing the self-governed island as Beijing’s redline issue and blasting “external connivance and support”, in another clear reference to the US.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed by law to supplying it with weapons.
Xie rattled off a host of trade, economic and social statistics, positing that China was open for business and welcomed imports from overseas partners, although it just recorded its largest trade surplus in history as it exports overcapacity and protectionism grows.
“Cooperation is better than a zero-sum game and stability is better than volatility, be it a tariff war, a trade war or a tech war. Each will hit a dead end and serve no one’s interest,” he said.
“When China and the United States work together, the world fares well. But if we confront one another, the world would suffer. We are sincere about developing China-US relations. At the same time, we have our principles to safeguard.”

data.title: Hong Kong’s Chris Tang defends police’s ‘difficult decision’ to shoot knife-wielding man
data.description:
Hong Kong’s security chief has defended police’s “difficult decision” to shoot a man who brandished a knife and took a woman hostage in a shopping centre, praising their bravery and decisiveness in saving lives.
After police fatally shot the 34-year-old man on Thursday, Security Secretary Chris Tang Ping-keung revealed the suspect was mentally unstable at the time, possibly influenced by drugs, with suspected substances found in his possession.
“It is obvious that [the suspect] was acting insane at the time,” Tang told a radio programme on Friday, adding the critical call had saved lives and stopped the suspect from hurting residents.
“It was a very difficult decision to shoot, but they were very brave. They were very decisive and I think our regular training is highly relevant to it.”
The shooting unfolded around 7pm on Thursday as the 34-year-old man broke into Don Don Donki supermarket in the centre and grabbed a knife from a kitchen counter. He waved at pedestrians, sparking panic as crowds fled.
An officer fired a burst of pepper spray, but the suspect refused to drop the weapon.
Video clips show him rushing the crowds at the centre exit, snatching a woman, grabbing her bag and holding her against his chest.
The force said that after a second verbal warning was ignored, two officers each fired one shot at the man, one hitting his chest, the other his right shoulder.
Tang said the officers were able to aim and shoot at the suspect’s body in such a critical moment thanks to force rehearsals and training on handling emergencies, especially where a suspect is about to stab a victim.

The woman was rescued and taken to hospital for treatment. The suspect received first aid at the scene before being rushed to Tuen Mun Hospital, where he was pronounced dead at 8.40pm.
The Post contacted the Social Service Department to ask if the suspect was an active case under its monitoring.
As a Post reporter visited Tuen Mun Plaza earlier in the day, the centre had blocked the shooting scene with large boards, while the exit to the site remained sealed off until around 10.40am.
A sign reading “Closed”, meanwhile, was hung at the Don Don Donki supermarket kitchen, where an online video allegedly showed a man grabbing a knife.
The kitchen had an open design, accessible through a waist-level door from the front or back.
An 18-year-old man, surnamed Kan, who lives and works nearby, said he was inside the shopping centre when the shooting occurred.
“I only heard gunshots. I had no idea what was going on at the time,” he said. “It was only after seeing the news that I learned someone was brandishing a knife and trying to stab someone,” he said.
Food and beverage worker Kay Lau, 33, who works in the area, did not witness the incident but urged colleagues to stay alert after hearing about it.
“I was really worried for my colleagues last night and called them to check; thankfully, they are safe,” he said. “This downtown area is supposed to be safe; no one expected something like this to happen.”
Lau said that while he generally felt safe in the neighbourhood, he had noticed a decline in law and order, hoping police would increase patrols.
